title: Build a Pendulum Model
description: Construct a simple pendulum, measure its period for different lengths and amplitudes, and compare your data to the theoretical prediction T = 2pi*sqrt(L/g).
whyItMatters: The simple pendulum is one of the most fundamental experiments in physics. It teaches measurement technique, data analysis, error propagation, and the comparison of theory to experiment — all in a single, elegant setup.

steps.title: Build the pendulum
steps.description: Attach a small dense mass (e.g. a metal nut) to a thin, inextensible string. Clamp the top end securely to a fixed support so it can swing freely.
steps.tips: Use a long string (1 m+) for easier timing. Minimise air resistance by using a compact, heavy mass.

steps.title: Measure period vs length
steps.description: For at least 6 different string lengths (0.2 m to 1.5 m), measure the period by timing 20 complete oscillations and dividing by 20. Keep the amplitude small (< 15 degrees).
steps.tips: Timing 20 oscillations reduces the fractional uncertainty in your period measurement by a factor of 20.

steps.title: Plot and analyse
steps.description: Plot T² vs L. According to theory, this should be a straight line with gradient 4pi²/g. Fit a straight line and extract g from the gradient.
steps.tips: Include error bars on your plot. Use a least-squares fit if you know how.

steps.title: Investigate amplitude dependence
steps.description: Fix the length and measure the period for amplitudes from 5 to 60 degrees. At what angle does the small-angle approximation break down?
steps.tips: You should see the period increase noticeably above ~30 degrees.

steps.title: Write up results
steps.description: Report your measured value of g with uncertainty. Compare to the accepted value (9.81 m/s²). Discuss sources of systematic error.
steps.tips: Common systematics: string stretching, air resistance, timing reaction time, pivot friction.

deliverables: A data table of period vs length with uncertainties
A T² vs L plot with line of best fit
A calculated value of g with propagated uncertainty
A discussion of amplitude dependence and the breakdown of the small-angle approximation
tools: Graphing software (Excel, Google Sheets, or Python/matplotlib)

rubric.description: At least 6 data points with proper uncertainties and repeated measurements
Correct T² vs L plot, proper line fit, accurate extraction of g
Uncertainty propagated correctly, systematic errors identified and discussed
Clear write-up with labelled graphs, units on all quantities, and a conclusion
